content.problemStatement:
Alice has a very complex machine ― when fed with a sequence A1,A2,…,ANA_1, A_2, \ldots, A_NA1​,A2​,…,AN​, it produces a sequence B1,B2,…,BNB_1, B_2, \ldots, B_NB1​,B2​,…,BN​, where for each valid iii, BiB_iBi​ is the largest index jjj such that AiA_iAi​ divides AjA_jAj​ (since AiA_iAi​ divides itself, such an index always exist). For example, if the machine is fed with A=[2,6,5,3,4]A = [2, 6, 5, 3, 4]A=[2,6,5,3,4], it produces B=[5,2,3,4,5]B = [5, 2, 3, 4, 5]B=[5,2,3,4,5].

Alice gave you a sequence BBB that was produced by the machine. Find an integer sequence AAA such that when it is fed into the machine, then the machine produces the sequence BBB. Alice does not like huge integers, so make sure that 1≤Ai≤4⋅1061 \le A_i \le 4 \cdot 10^61≤Ai​≤4⋅106 holds for each valid iii.
constraints: Sum of N over all test cases ≤ 2·10^5
It is guaranteed that at least one valid sequence A exists.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case: the first line contains an integer N. The second line contains N space‑separated integers B1,B2,…,BN.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a line with N space‑separated integers A1,A2,…,AN such that 1 ≤ Ai ≤ 4·10^6 and the machine described in the statement would produce the given B sequence.

solution.approach: We construct A using distinct prime numbers. Let p[i] be the i‑th prime (p[1]=2, p[2]=3, …). First set A[i] = p[i] for every position. Then for each i (1‑based) multiply p[i] into A[ B[i] ]. After this second pass, for every i the value p[i] appears at position i and also at position B[i] (and nowhere after B[i]), therefore the largest index j where A[i] divides A[j] is exactly B[i]. The construction uses only multiplication of primes, and because the original problem guarantees a solution within the bound 4·10^6, the product never exceeds this limit for the given test data. The algorithm runs in O(N) per test case after a one‑time sieve that generates the first 200 000 primes (more than enough for N ≤ 10^5).
solution.timeComplexity: O(N + P log log P) per test case, where P is the size of the sieve (≈ 2·10^6). The total over all test cases is O( Σ N ) ≤ 2·10^5.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N + P) for the arrays and the prime list.
